senses.definition: Actively trying to get power, success, or an important position.
examples: Several young managers are ambitioning the top job in the company.
She was openly ambitioning a seat in parliament.
He spent years ambitioning a role in senior leadership.

senses.definition: Someone who is extremely ambitious and strongly focused on reaching high goals.
examples: The team is full of young ambitionists dreaming of international success.
As an ambitionist, she never stopped training for the national squad.
Some see him as a visionary, others as a ruthless ambitionist.

senses.definition: Having no strong wish to succeed or to achieve anything special.
examples: He felt completely ambitionless after losing his job.
The character in the novel is portrayed as lazy and ambitionless.
An ambitionless attitude can hold you back from new opportunities.

senses.definition: In a way that shows no desire to achieve or improve anything.
examples: He drifted ambitionlessly from job to job.
The students sat ambitionlessly through the lecture.
She moved through her twenties rather ambitionlessly, unsure of what she wanted.

senses.definition: Strong desires to achieve something, especially success in work or life.
examples: She has big ambitions for her career.
His parents encouraged his ambitions to study abroad.
Their political ambitions eventually led them into government.

senses.definition: Having a strong wish to be successful, powerful, or to achieve something important.
examples: She is an ambitious young lawyer who works long hours.
He has always been very ambitious and wants to start his own company.
Their most ambitious student won a scholarship overseas.

senses.definition: Needing a lot of time, effort, or skill to succeed.
examples: It is an ambitious project that will take years to complete.
They set an ambitious goal of cutting emissions by half.
Writing the novel in six months was an ambitious plan.

senses.definition: In a way that shows a strong wish to succeed or reach difficult goals.
examples: The company is ambitiously expanding into new markets.
She ambitiously applied for several senior positions.
The film ambitiously tries to cover fifty years of history.

senses.definition: The quality of having a strong desire to succeed or to achieve important things.
examples: Her ambitiousness impressed the interview panel.
There is a fine line between ambitiousness and greed.
His ambitiousness drove the project forward.

senses.definition: Plural of ambitiousness; different forms or examples of people’s strong desire to succeed.
examples: The novel contrasts the ambitiousnesses of its main characters.
Different cultural values shape the ambitiousnesses seen in global leaders.
She studied the various ambitiousnesses that appear in entrepreneurial life stories.

senses.definition: Plural of ambit; the limits or range of something such as power, activity, or interest.
examples: These issues fall within the ambits of criminal law.
The research goes beyond the traditional ambits of sociology.
The court examined the legal ambits of the new regulation.

senses.definition: Of glass, having stony speckles that show it has started to lose its clear, smooth form.
examples: The glassblower discarded the ambitty piece as defective.
Ambitty glass can weaken the structure of a finished vase.
They studied how cooling speed leads to ambitty surfaces in glass.

senses.definition: In law or history, the practice of bribing voters or using unfair pressure to win public office in ancient Rome.
examples: Ambitus was a serious crime in the political life of the Roman Republic.
Some historians compare modern corruption scandals to Roman ambitus.
The senator was accused of ambitus after reports of gifts to voters.

senses.definition: In music theory, the full range of notes used in a melody or in a vocal part.
examples: The chant has a narrow ambitus that makes it easy to sing.
Composers sometimes restrict the ambitus of a theme for effect.
The soprano line shows an unusually wide ambitus.

senses.definition: A state of having mixed or opposing feelings about someone or something.
examples: She felt deep ambivalence about taking the promotion abroad.
His ambivalence toward marriage confused his partner.
Public ambivalence makes it hard to pass the new law.

senses.definition: Plural of ambivalence; different cases or types of mixed or opposing feelings.
examples: The book explores the ambivalences in modern family life.
Her diary reveals the many ambivalences she felt about success.
Social media can create new ambivalences about privacy.

senses.definition: Plural form of ambivalency; several instances or patterns of mixed feelings toward something.
examples: The study identified multiple ambivalencies in teenagers’ views on work.
Writers have long described the ambivalencies of fame.
Different cultures show different ambivalencies about individual freedom.

senses.definition: The state of having mixed or opposing feelings about someone or something; another form of ambivalence.
examples: Her ambivalency toward the job offer was clear in the interview.
Therapists often meet ambivalency about change in their clients.
The article discusses public ambivalency about new technologies.

senses.definition: Having or showing mixed or opposing feelings about someone or something.
examples: She is ambivalent about moving to another country.
He felt ambivalent towards his new boss: impressed yet suspicious.
Many people are ambivalent about the impact of social media.

senses.definition: In a way that shows mixed or opposing feelings.
examples: She smiled ambivalently, unsure whether to feel happy or sad.
The public reacted ambivalently to the announcement.
He spoke ambivalently about returning to his hometown.

senses.definition: A personality style in which someone shows both introverted and extroverted qualities, depending on the situation.
examples: Ambiversion allows people to enjoy both social events and quiet time alone.
The study suggests that ambiversion may be linked to greater emotional balance.
She recognised her ambiversion when she noticed how differently she behaved at work and at home.

senses.definition: Plural of ambiversion; different examples or degrees of a personality style that mixes introversion and extraversion.
examples: Researchers described several ambiversions found in the sample.
The book outlines the many ambiversions that appear in everyday life.
Different ambiversions can be seen in how people handle stress.

senses.definition: Having a personality that shows both introverted and extroverted characteristics.
examples: Ambiversive individuals adapt easily to both group and solo work.
Her ambiversive nature meant she enjoyed parties but also needed quiet weekends.
The training helped ambiversive employees use their strengths in different roles.

senses.definition: A person whose personality is between introvert and extrovert and changes with the situation.
examples: As an ambivert, he enjoys parties but also needs time alone to recharge.
Many people are ambiverts rather than clear introverts or extroverts.
The test result labelled her an ambivert with a slight lean toward introversion.

senses.definition: Plural of ambivert; people whose personalities are between introvert and extrovert.
examples: Ambiverts often feel comfortable in many social situations.
The study found that ambiverts formed the largest group of participants.
In the workplace, ambiverts can switch between leading and listening.

senses.definition: To walk in a slow, relaxed way.
examples: They amble through the park every Sunday morning.
We ambled along the river, chatting about nothing in particular.
Tourists were ambling around the old town square.

senses.definition: A slow, relaxed walk.
examples: We took an amble around the lake before dinner.
Their evening amble became a daily habit.
He went for a gentle amble to clear his head.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of amble: walked in a slow, relaxed way.
examples: We ambled back to the hotel after lunch.
She ambled over to say hello.
They ambled through the market, stopping at every stall.

senses.definition: A scientific name used in botany for a particular genus or group of plants.
examples: The researcher specialised in the classification of Ambleocarpus.
Specimens of Ambleocarpus were collected during the field trip.
Few gardeners are familiar with Ambleocarpus outside academic circles.

senses.definition: A person who walks in a slow, relaxed way.
examples: The park fills with amblers on sunny afternoons.
As a natural ambler, he prefers slow walks to intense hikes.
City amblers enjoyed the car‑free streets.

senses.definition: Plural of ambler; people who walk slowly in a relaxed way.
examples: Amblers and cyclists shared the riverside path.
On weekends, the park is full of amblers enjoying the sunshine.
The narrow street was crowded with tourists and amblers.

senses.definition: Third person singular of amble: walks in a slow, relaxed way.
examples: She ambles down to the café every morning.
The dog ambles happily beside its owner.
He ambles around the garden after dinner.

senses.definition: Plural of amble: several slow, relaxed walks.
examples: Our weekend ambles became a cherished routine.
He enjoys long ambles in the countryside.
Their gentle ambles gave them time to talk.

senses.definition: Walking in a slow, relaxed way.
examples: They were ambling along the beach at sunset.
We spent the afternoon ambling around the old town.
An elderly couple came ambling down the lane.

senses.definition: The activity or style of walking in a slow, relaxed way.
examples: Their gentle ambling gave them time to enjoy the scenery.
We spent an hour in pleasant ambling before lunch.
Her ambling was too slow for the rest of the group.

senses.definition: In a slow, relaxed walking manner.
examples: They moved amblingly through the crowded market.
The story progresses amblingly, without any real tension.
He walked amblingly across the field, in no hurry to arrive.

senses.definition: Plural of ambling; several occasions of walking slowly and in a relaxed way.
examples: Our quiet amblings through the village became a daily pleasure.
His holiday photos show his aimless amblings along the coast.
She wrote about her amblings in the countryside.

senses.definition: A rare medical term related to abnormal development or softening of tissue.
examples: The condition, known as amblosis, was described in early medical texts.
Doctors rarely use the term amblosis in modern practice.
Researchers reviewed historical cases of amblosis in the literature.

senses.definition: Relating to or affected by amblosis, a rare tissue condition.
examples: Amblotic changes were observed in the muscle tissue.
The report described several amblotic lesions.
Such amblotic processes are now extremely rare.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which a person’s hearing is weaker than normal.
examples: Amblyacousia can make it hard to follow conversations in noisy rooms.
The patient was diagnosed with mild amblyacousia in the left ear.
Early testing for amblyacousia allows for better support at school.

senses.definition: A medical term for reduced sense of touch.
examples: The nerve damage resulted in amblyaphia in her fingertips.
Patients with amblyaphia may not notice minor injuries.
Doctors tested for amblyaphia using light pressure on the skin.

senses.definition: A scientific family name used in zoology for a group of animals with broad or blunt heads.
examples: The family Amblycephalidae includes several small snake species.
Students compared Amblycephalidae with related families.
New fossils were tentatively placed in Amblycephalidae.

senses.definition: A scientific genus name used in zoology for certain animals with broad or blunt heads.
examples: Amblycephalus is rarely mentioned outside specialist zoological texts.
The specimen was originally assigned to Amblycephalus.
Researchers revised the description of Amblycephalus based on new data.

senses.definition: In optics, describing a lens or system with only limited correction for colour errors.
examples: The early telescope used an amblychromatic lens.
Amblychromatic designs can cause slight colour fringes at the image edge.
Engineers replaced the amblychromatic element with a modern one.

senses.definition: A scientific name used in zoology for a group of animals with short or blunt fingers or toes.
examples: Amblydactyla has been proposed as a separate genus in some classifications.
The fossil showed features typical of Amblydactyla.
Researchers debated whether the species belonged in Amblydactyla.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which the sense of taste is weaker than normal.
examples: Patients with amblygeusia may complain that food tastes bland.
The doctor considered medication as a possible cause of the amblygeusia.
Amblygeusia can be an early sign of some neurological diseases.

senses.definition: An old term in geometry for an obtuse‑angled triangle or figure with an angle greater than a right angle.
examples: The textbook uses the term amblygon for an obtuse triangle.
Students were asked to identify the amblygon in the diagram.
In older works, an obtuse triangle is sometimes called an amblygon.

senses.definition: Having one or more angles greater than a right angle.
examples: The amblygonal shape made the calculations more complex.
Designers used an amblygonal form for the building’s main hall.
An amblygonal triangle has one obtuse angle.

senses.definition: A mineral that contains lithium, used as an ore of lithium and sometimes as a gemstone.
examples: Amblygonite is mined as a minor source of lithium.
The jeweller showed us a pale green amblygonite stone.
Geologists identified amblygonite in the pegmatite rock.

senses.definition: Plural of amblygonite; several pieces or types of this lithium‑bearing mineral.
examples: The museum displays several amblygonites from different regions.
They analysed amblygonites to measure lithium content.
Jewellers sometimes cut amblygonites as collector stones.

senses.definition: In botany, having fruit that develops slowly or poorly.
examples: Some mosses are described as amblyocarpous in older texts.
The amblyocarpous species produces few mature capsules.
Researchers compared amblyocarpous plants with more fertile ones.

senses.definition: A scientific genus name for a group of hard ticks, some of which can spread disease.
examples: Amblyomma ticks are common in tropical regions.
Researchers studied Amblyomma as a vector of several illnesses.
The bite was identified as coming from an Amblyomma species.

senses.definition: A person who has amblyopia, or lazy eye.
examples: As an amblyope, she needed regular eye exercises in childhood.
The clinic runs support groups for amblyopes.
Researchers studied how amblyopes respond to visual training.

senses.definition: A childhood eye condition in which one or both eyes have poorer vision because they did not develop normally, often called lazy eye.
examples: Early treatment of amblyopia can prevent permanent vision loss.
The child wore a patch over the good eye to treat amblyopia.
Amblyopia is a common cause of reduced sight in children.

senses.definition: Plural of amblyopia; different cases or types of the lazy eye condition.
examples: The clinic specialises in treating various amblyopias.
Researchers compared amblyopias caused by different factors.
Most amblyopias can be improved if detected early.
